title: Baricitinib + teplizumab dual immunomodulation to preserve beta cell mass in newly diagnosed T1D
description: Combining baricitinib (JAK1/JAK2 inhibitor, already FDA-approved for RA) with teplizumab (anti-CD3 antibody, FDA-approved as Tzield for T1D delay) in newly diagnosed T1D patients could preserve greater than 80% of remaining beta cell function. Baricitinib has shown C-peptide preservation in the BANDIT trial, while teplizumab modulates the autoimmune attack. Together, they target different arms of the immune response: teplizumab depletes and exhausts autoreactive T cells, while baricitinib blocks the JAK-STAT inflammatory signaling that recruits new immune effectors.
reasoning: Each drug individually has shown partial beta cell preservation. Teplizumab delayed T1D onset by 2+ years in at-risk individuals (landmark TrialNet TN-10 study) and is now FDA-approved. Baricitinib showed preserved C-peptide in the BANDIT trial (phase 2, n=91). The hypothesis is that combining both could be synergistic rather than merely additive, because they target complementary immune pathways. Risk: combining two immunomodulators increases infection risk; no combination trial data exists yet; the window for intervention is narrow (must be given soon after diagnosis while beta cells remain).

evidence.title: BANDIT trial: Baricitinib preserves beta cell function in new-onset T1D
evidence.content: Phase 2 RCT (n=91) showed baricitinib-treated patients had significantly higher stimulated C-peptide levels at 48 weeks compared to placebo. Mean daily insulin dose was 49% lower in the baricitinib group.

evidence.title: Teplizumab delays T1D onset in at-risk individuals by median 2 years
evidence.content: TrialNet TN-10 study demonstrated that a single 14-day course of teplizumab delayed clinical T1D diagnosis by a median of 2 years in high-risk individuals with 2+ autoantibodies. Led to FDA approval as Tzield.

approach: Dual immunomodulation for beta cell preservation at diagnosis
